coredata.dc:description: Oxytetracycline (OTC), a broad class of antibacterial medication, has been widely utilized in poultry husbandry and fish farming. However, the presence of OTC residues in the food has become a matter of great concern. These residues have the potential to induce the emergence and spread of bacterial resistance, which can have far-reaching consequences for public health. In this work, we focused on the comparison of the quenching efficiency of UiO-66 and its amino group-modified counterpart UiO-66-NH2, towards the fluorescence of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) labeled aptamer (NGQDs-apt). It was found that UiO-66-NH2 demonstrated a more potent fluorescence quenching ability towards NGQDs-apt, as high as 98.8 %, compared to 69.7 % for UiO-66 without amino functionalization. This enhanced performance is attributed to the improved interaction between the amino groups on UiO-66-NH2 and the NGQDs-apt, facilitating more effective electron transfer. Consequently, UiO-66-NH2 was selected as the optimal quencher for the development of a highly sensitive aptasensor. This aptasensor demonstrates a quantitative detection range for OTC spanning from 0.001 to 200 μM with an exceptionally low detection limit of 0.25 nM, showing simplicity, accuracy, stability and great potential for antibiotic residue detection in food samples. This work addresses the urgent need to develop reliable methods for detecting OTC residues in food due to the potential of OTC to trigger bacterial resistance, which is crucial for food safety and public health.

head.ce:title: Enhanced quenching efficiency of UiO-66-NH2 over UiO-66 to engineer high-performance fluorescence aptasensor for oxytetracycline monitoring

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oxytetracycline (OTC), a broad class of antibacterial medication, has been widely utilized in poultry husbandry and fish farming. However, the presence of OTC residues in the food has become a matter of great concern. These residues have the potential to induce the emergence and spread of bacterial resistance, which can have far-reaching consequences for public health. In this work, we focused on the comparison of the quenching efficiency of UiO-66 and its amino group-modified counterpart UiO-66-NH2, towards the fluorescence of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) labeled aptamer (NGQDs-apt). It was found that UiO-66-NH2 demonstrated a more potent fluorescence quenching ability towards NGQDs-apt, as high as 98.8 %, compared to 69.7 % for UiO-66 without amino functionalization. This enhanced performance is attributed to the improved interaction between the amino groups on UiO-66-NH2 and the NGQDs-apt, facilitating more effective electron transfer. Consequently, UiO-66-NH2 was selected as the optimal quencher for the development of a highly sensitive aptasensor. This aptasensor demonstrates a quantitative detection range for OTC spanning from 0.001 to 200 μM with an exceptionally low detection limit of 0.25 nM, showing simplicity, accuracy, stability and great potential for antibiotic residue detection in food samples. This work addresses the urgent need to develop reliable methods for detecting OTC residues in food due to the potential of OTC to trigger bacterial resistance, which is crucial for food safety and public health.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxytetracycline (OTC), a broad class of antibacterial medication, has been widely utilized in poultry husbandry and fish farming due to its high antibacterial efficacy, cost-effectiveness, and relatively low incidence of adverse effects [1]. Nevertheless, the existence of OTC residues in the environment poses a significant concern as it can potentially trigger the development of bacterial resistance [2]. Moreover, the consumption of food products contaminated with OTC residues by humans may result in the accumulation of OTC within the body, leading to adverse damage to the human body [3]. China's national food safety standard (GB 31650-2019) states that residual levels of OTC in poultry shall not exceed 200 μg kg−1. Chicken is rich in protein and has low levels of fat and cholesterol. China plays a significant role in the global chicken market, ranking third in production and second in consumption worldwide, contributing 14.30 % of global output and 14.72 % of global consumption. Given the rising chicken consumption and the growing issue of antibiotic misuse in poultry, developing a simple, rapid, and sensitive method for detecting OTC residues in chicken is of great importance [4].

Conventional methods for detecting OTC residues include instrumental analysis [5,6], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [7], and aptasensors [8]. Instrumental methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography and liquid chromatography-mass spectroscopy are renowned for their excellent sensitivity, robust anti-interference capacity, and high accuracy [9–11]. However, they also have limitations, such as expensive instrumentation and complex sample preparation, limiting their broad use [12,13]. ELISA offers advantages including portability, ease of use, high sensitivity, and excellent specificity, making it particularly effective in high-throughput tests [14]. However, the difficulty and high cost associated with antibody production has limited their applications [15,16]. Compared to traditional antibodies, aptamers have advantages such as high affinity, ease of production, low cost, easy modification, and high stability, making them widely applicable in various fields [17–21]. In recent years, there have been some fluorescence aptasensors reported for the detection of antibiotics due to the advantages of fluorescence analysis such as high sensitivity, good simplicity, and rapidity in operation [22–24]. For instance, a fluorescent aptasensor employing DNA four-arm junctions-assisted target recycling and SYBR Green I-aided fluorescence-signal amplification, enables sensitive detection of chloramphenicol with potential for food analysis [25]. An unlabeled fluorescence aptsensor was developed based on G-quadruplex/thioflavin T complex, which can enable the quick on-site detection of florfenicol [26]. This aptasensor has high specificity and practicality, with a wide detection range and detection time of about 10 min. However, greater efforts are still needed to develop more sensitive fluorescence aptasensors for the detection of antibiotic residues.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have wide use in biomedicine, electrocatalysis, energy storage, and biosensors due to their porous framework structure, high surface area, tunable pore sizes, and excellent chemical stability [27–29]. MOFs always possess organic linkers on their surfaces, which are characterized by the presence of conjugated π-electronic systems [30]. Such conjugated π-electronic systems can not only help to tune the structure of MOFs but also serve to enhance the functionality of MOFs. MOFs equipped with organic linkers are capable of interacting with fluorophore-labeled single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) through π-π stacking interactions, consequently resulting in the effective quenching of the fluorophore's fluorescence [31]. Given this advantage, these MOFs abundant in π-electrons can thus be exploited as a powerful tool to fulfill the objective of the development of fluorescence aptasensors [32]. Compared to other MOFs, UiO-66, a representative Zr-MOF, has a large specific surface area while the unsaturated coordination zirconium in UiO-66 exhibits a good affinity towards carboxyl groups [33], thereby promoting more efficient capture and immobilization of biomolecules and consequently enhancing biosensing performance [34]. This capture capability could potentially be further improved through functionalization. Aptamers possess negative charge, making amino modification of materials to be an attractive approach in the preparation of aptamer adsorbents [35]. This fact has inspired us to explore the introduction of amino groups onto the surface of UiO-66 to improve the performance of UiO-66-based fluorescence aptasensors.

Herein, we focused on the comparison of UiO-66 and its amino group-modified counterpart, UiO-66-NH2, with the goal of developing a highly efficient fluorescence aptasensor for OTC detection in chicken. Amino groups were intended to incorporate into UiO-66 with the intention of enhancing the capture capacity towards aptamer and consequently enhance the fluorescence quenching efficiency of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) labeled aptamer (NGQDs-apt) attached on UiO-66-NH2. As expected, UiO-66-NH2 demonstrated a more potent fluorescence quenching ability towards NGQDs-apt, as high as 98.8 % compared to 69.7 % for UiO-66 without amino functionalization. On this basis, we have developed a high-performance fluorescence aptasensor for OTC monitoring based on the excellent fluorescence quenching ability of UiO-66-NH2 towards NGQDs-apt.

We obtained ZrCl4, 2-aminoterephthalic acid (2-APDC), terephthalic acid (BDC), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), urea, citric acid, methanol, ethanol, OTC, tetracycline (TET), doxycycline (DOX), lomefloxacin (LOM), ofloxacin (OFLX), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC), and N-hydroxy succinimide (NHS) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The aptamer for OTC (5′–NH2–CGT ACG GAA TTC GCT AGC GGG CGG GGG TGC TGG GGG AAT GGA GTG CTG CGT GCT GCG GGG ATC CGA GCT CCA CGT G-3′) was synthesized by Shanghai Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (China). The aptasensor was prepared and tested using a 10 mM Tris-HCl buffer at pH 7.4. All experiments were conducted with deionized water.

The morphologies of the samples were characterized using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, MIRA, TESCAN, The Czech Republic). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were measured using a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a Nicolet Nexus 470 FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo Nicolet, USA).

UiO-66 was prepared following a reported work [36]. 1.137 mmol of ZrCl4 and 1.137 mmol of BDC were dissolved in the mixture composed of DMF (45 mL) and acetic acid (5 mL), and then sonicated for 30 min, which was sealed into a teflon-lined autoclave and treated at 120 °C for 24 h. The products were washed thrice using centrifugation with DMF and ethanol (8000 rpm for 5 min), which were dried under vacuum in an oven at 120 °C for 10 h, and stored at 4 °C for later use.

UiO-66-NH2 was prepared using a reported method [37]. Both ZrCl4 (2.4 mmol) and 2-APDC (2.4 mmol) were dissolved separately in 30 mL of DMF with thorough stirring. Next, we mixed them under stirring, followed by adding 15 mL of acetic acid and sonicated the mixture for 20 min, which was sealed into a teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 120 °C for 24 h. The solid products were separated by centrifugation and washed three times with DMF and methanol each (8000 rpm for 5 min). The resultant products were then dispersed in methanol at 60 °C for three days to further purify. After centrifugation, the resultant UiO-66-NH2 was dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 24 h and stored at 4 °C for later use.

NGQDs were prepared using a hydrothermal approach [38]. Initially, 20 mL of citric acid (40 mg mL−1) and urea (7.2 mg mL−1) were mixed and placed to a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted for 8 h at 180 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the products were transferred to a beaker with the pH adjusting to 7.0 using NaOH (5 mg mL−1). An excess of ethanol was introduced and allowed to stand for 15 min. Then centrifugation was performed at 13,000 rpm for 5 min to collect the NGQDs, which was re-dispersed in 20 mL of water and stored at 4 °C for future use.

To couple NGQDs with aptamers, a mixture of NGQDs solution (5 mL), EDC solution (100 μL, 200 μM), and NHS solution (100 μL, 200 μM) was stirred for 2 h to activate the carboxyl groups on the surface of the NGQDs. Subsequently, 20 μL of aptamer solution was added to above mixture and incubated overnight at room temperature. The resulting aptamer modified NGQDs (NGQDs-apt) probes were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and washed three times with Tris-HCl buffer. Finally, the collected NGQDs-apt probes were re-dispersed in 5 mL of Tris-HCl buffer and stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

ce:section-title.ce:italic: Quenching efficiency of UiO-66 and UiO-66-NH

ce:section.ce:para: 20 μL of NGQDs-apt was added to 80 μL of Tris-HCl buffer containing various concentrations (0.50, 0.75, 1.00, 1.50, and 2.00 mg mL−1) of UiO-66 or UiO-66-NH2 dispersions. After thoroughly vortexing, we added 100 μL of Tris-HCl buffer and incubated it at 37 °C on a shaker for 1 h. The supernatant was collected for fluorescence measurements after centrifugation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the fluorescence aptasensor and detection procedure
ce:section.ce:para: Incubation of 100 μL NGQDs-apt with 300 μL UiO-66-NH2 (1.00 mg mL−1) and reacted for 8 min at 37 °C. After centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min and washing with Tris-HCl buffer for three times, the NGQDs-apt attached on UiO-66-NH2 (NGQDs-apt/UiO-66-NH2) bioconjugations were collected and then re-dispersed in 5 mL of Tris-HCl buffer for further study. For OTC detection, we introduced 100 μL of OTC solution with different concentrations to 400 μL of NGQDs-apt/UiO-66-NH2 and incubated them on a shaker at 37 °C for 40 min. After centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min, we collected the supernatant containing the released NGQDs-apt fluorescence probes, which was diluted to an ultimate volume of 1 mL to record its fluorescence intensity at a 360 nm excitation wavelength.

Preparation of real chicken samples was referred to the reported work [39]. The slaughtered chicken was purchased from a local poultry market. The slaughter process included stunning followed by exsanguination, following local regulations for food safety and poultry welfare. Then 0.5 g of fresh chicken breast was processed into minced meat, which was placed in 1 mL ethyl acetate and sonicated for 30 min and then was centrifuged for 10 min at 10000 rpm. The supernatant was diluted 10-fold with Tris-HCl buffer, followed by the addition of OTC standard solution with the final concentration of 0, 5, 8, and 10 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working principle of the fluorescence aptasensor for OTC monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: UiO-66-NH2 was synthesized utilizing a solvothermal method (Scheme 1 A), while NGQDs were produced via a hydrothermal approach (Scheme 1B). The fluorescence probe NGQDs-apt, was prepared by conjugating carboxyl groups present on the surface of NGQDs with amino groups that had been modified onto the aptamer. The fabrication procedure and the working principle of the designed fluorescence aptasensor were outlined as Scheme 1C. In the absence of OTC, through π-π stacking interaction, hydrogen bonding, and the electrostatic attraction between the phosphate backbone of the aptamer and the amino groups on UiO-66-NH2, the fluorescence of NGQDs was quenched by UiO-66-NH2. In the presence of OTC, the NGQDs-apt exhibited a stronger affinity for binding to its target, OTC, causing the fluorescence probe to detach from UiO-66-NH2. This detachment led to a subsequent recovery of the NGQDs' fluorescence. Based on this mechanism, a correlation was established between the changes in fluorescence intensity and the concentrations of OTC, which could be used for OTC monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of the resultant UiO-66 and UiO-66-NH2
ce:section.ce:para: We examined the morphologies of the obtained UiO-66 and UiO-66-NH2 using SEM images. The results confirmed that both UiO-66 (Fig. 1 A) and UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. 1B) exhibited a regular octahedral structure, with good dispersion and uniform size distribution. Specifically, the particle size of UiO-66 was approximately 200 nm, while that of UiO-66-NH2 was around 105 nm. For UiO-66 (Fig. 1C), the energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS) mapping images illustrated the identification of the elements C, O, and Zr, with the N signal at a level similar to the background noise. The EDS mapping images for UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. 1D) revealed the elements C, O, Zr, and N, indicating that the NH2 groups were successfully incorporated onto UiO-66. The composition of UiO-66 (Fig. 1E) and UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. 1F) was further confirmed using EDS spectroscopy. The results showed that both samples included the components C, O, and Zr. As could be seen from Table S1, the content of N in UiO-66-NH2 (4.49 %) was much greater than UiO-66 (0.68 %). The low amount of N in UiO-66 might be due to inadequate removal of DMF solvent during centrifugal washing, resulting in residues within the material. These finding confirmed the difference in chemical compositions between UiO-66 and UiO-66-NH2.

The functional groups of UiO-66 and UiO-66-NH2 have been investigated by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 1G). UiO-66 (curve a) exhibited symmetric and asymmetric O–C–O carboxylate stretching in BDC, demonstrated by the bands at 1381 and 1572 cm−1 [40]. The distinct bands at 1496 and 1658 cm−1 indicated the ligand's C–C benzene ring and C–O stretching vibrations, respectively. The prominent bands at 653, 715 and 764 cm−1 could be linked to the Zr–O modes and C–H/O–H bonds in the ligand [41]. The bands at 970 and 1159 cm−1 correspond to Zr-BDC coordination and residual DMF, accordingly [42,43]. UiO-66-NH2 (curve b) exhibited C–N stretching bands at 1257 and 1335 cm−1, while CO band at 1659 cm−1 and a single (N–H) band at 3355 cm−1 [44]. The secondary amides observed at 1572 and 764 cm−1 indicating N–H wagging [45]. In Fig. S1, two absorption bands at 280 and 360 nm were observed for both UiO-66 (curve a) and UiO-66-NH2 (curve b). The absorption band in the 300–400 nm range was corresponded to the n-π∗ transition of the lone pair electron on the NH2 group of the ligand. Compared to UiO-66, UiO-66-NH2 exhibited higher peak intensities in these bands, which confirmed the successful amino functionalization of UiO-66. To gain insights into the surface pore structure of both UiO-66 and UiO-66-NH2, the nitrogen adsorption−desorption isotherm (Fig. S2) was analyzed, revealing atypical type II isotherm, confirming their microporous nature [46]. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area measurements (Table S2) showed that the specific surface area of UiO-66-NH2 (1085.7 m2 g−1) was 1.5 times higher than that of UiO-66 (746.1 m2 g−1), suggesting that the introduction of amino groups might change the surface properties of UiO-66.

The emission wavelength of NGQDs demonstrated a nearly excitation-independent characteristic within the range of 300–380 nm (Fig. 2 A). Specifically, it exhibited an optimal excitation wavelength at 360 nm, accompanied by the strongest emission peak at 444 nm. Furthermore, the resultant NGQDs displayed a light-yellow color under sunlight (photograph a, Fig. 2B) and emitted blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (photograph b, Fig. 2B). The fluorescence spectrum of the synthesized NGQDs was translated into the corresponding chromaticity coordinates and plotted on the CIE 1931 color space chromaticity diagram. As shown in Fig. 2C, the red circle (B), representing the chromaticity coordinates of the NGQDs, was situated in the blue region, near the curved boundary, indicating that the emitted light of NGQDs was a relatively pure blue [47]. Surface functionalities played a crucial role in determining the biolabeling potential of NGQDs. FTIR spectra of NGQDs (Fig. 2D) and NGQDs-apt revealed peaks at 3376 cm−1 and 3214 cm−1, which corresponded to the presence of O–H and N–H bonds, respectively [48]. The peak at 1645 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of carboxyl groups, while the peak around 1400 cm−1 indicated C–N stretching vibrations [49]. Using the EDC/NHS chemistry, the carboxyl groups on the NGQDs' surfaces were cross-linked with the amino groups on the aptamer, resulting in the production of NGQDs-apt signal tags. CO antisymmetric stretching vibration absorption and CO symmetric stretching vibration absorption of NGQDs-apt (curve b, Fig. 2D) decreased compared to that of NGQDs (curve a, Fig. 2D), suggesting that aptamer was successfully covalently attached to NGQDs via amide bonding [50]. When compared to the fluorescence emission peak of NGQDs (curve a, Fig. 2E) at 447 nm, the emission peak of NGQDs-apt (curve b, Fig. 2E) showed a 10 nm blue shift. This blue shift was most likely due to the biomolecule conjugation on the surface of NGQDs. Furthermore, NGQDs-apt (curve b, Fig. 2F) displayed both the UV absorption peak of NGQDs (curve a, Fig. 2F) at 340 nm and the UV absorption peak of the aptamer at 260 nm, indicating the successful preparation of the NGQDs-apt fluorescence probes [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of UiO-66-NH2 and feasibility of the designed fluorescence aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the performance of UiO-66-NH2 as a fluorescence quenching material, we compared the quenching efficiency of diverse concentrations of MOFs towards NGQDs-apt. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the quenching efficiency based on UiO-66 and UiO-66-NH2 enhanced with the increasing of concentrations, reaching a plateau at 1.50 mg mL−1 of UiO-66 and 1.00 mg mL−1 of UiO-66-NH2. In addition, by comparing the fluorescence quenching rate of UiO-66 at 1.50 mg mL−1 and UiO-66-NH2 at 1.00 mg mL−1, the specific surface area of them was nearly the same, and the results showed that UiO-66-NH2 had a greater fluorescence quenching ability on NGQDs-apt, reaching 98.8 % compared to 69.7 % of UiO-66 without amino functionalization. Therefore, 1.00 mg mL−1 could be used as the optimal concentration of UiO-66-NH2 in subsequent experiments.

The feasibility of this "turn-on" fluorescence aptasensor for detecting OTC was illustrated in Fig. 3B. When excited at a wavelength of 360 nm, NGQDs-apt emitted a strong fluorescence at 435 nm (curve a, Fig. 3B). Upon introducing UiO-66-NH2 dispersion, the fluorescence intensity of NGQDs-apt decreased by 95.7 % (curve b, Fig. 3B). This might be attributed to the photo-induced electron transfer between NGQDs-apt and UiO-66-NH2, which led to the quenching of the fluorescence of NGQDs-apt [52]. The crystal structures of UiO-66 and UiO-66-NH2, as well as the processes of capturing and detaching NGQDs-apt, were presented in Fig. 3C. UiO-66 was capable of primarily adsorbing NGQDs-apt via π-π stacking interaction and hydrogen bonding, effectively reducing the fluorescence of NGQDs [53]. The results in Fig. S3 showed that UiO-66-NH2 had a positive charge (+18.7 mV), while the aptamer had a negative charge (−9.83 mV) due to its phosphate backbone. After conjugation, the zeta potential of the composite material shifted to a lower positive value (+8.83 mV), which confirmed that there was electrostatic interaction between aptamer and UiO-66-NH2 [54]. In the context of UiO-66-NH2, it was proposed that, in addition to the π-π stacking interaction and hydrogen bonding, the electrostatic attraction between the aptamer and the amino groups in UiO-66-NH2 also played a crucial role in promoting the adsorption of NGQDs-apt onto UiO-66-NH2 [55]. This could explain why UiO-66-NH2 quenched NGQDs-apt more efficiently than UiO-66. Subsequently, upon adding 10 nM of OTC resulted in the detachment of NGQDs-apt from the UiO-66-NH2 surface, interrupting the photo-induced electron transfer process between them. Consequently, the fluorescence intensity of NGQDs-apt was restored to 41.70 % of its original intensity (curve c, Fig. 3B), but through subsequent experiments, it was found that the fluorescence intensity of NGQDs-apt's recovery rate could reach a maximum of 75.75 % under the optimal detection conditions (Fig. 5A). This outcome verified the successful fabrication of a "turn-on" fluorescence aptasensor for detecting OTC.

Several key parameters were optimized to enhance the analytical performance of the aptasensor. Notably, the volume ratio between UiO-66-NH2 and NGQDs-apt was pivotal in facilitating efficient fluorescence quenching of NGQDs. The impact of this volume ratio on fluorescence quenching was assessed by reacting a fixed volume of NGQDs-apt (100 μL) with varying volumes of UiO-66-NH2 while maintaining a total solution volume of 700 μL using Tris-HCl buffer to ensure consistent experimental conditions. As indicated in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of NGQDs-apt/UiO-66-NH2 composites significantly decreased as the volume ratio increased, stabilizing at a ratio of 3:1. Consequently, a volume ratio of 3:1 was identified as optimal for producing NGQDs-apt/UiO-66-NH2 composites with high quenching efficiency. We further refined the adsorption time between UiO-66-NH2 and NGQDs-apt to facilitate rapid detection. A mixture was prepared containing 300 μL of UiO-66-NH2 dispersion and 100 μL of NGQDs-apt, with the total volume adjusted to 700 μL using Tris-HCl buffer. The fluorescence intensity of the supernatant was then measured at various time intervals to determine the optimal adsorption duration. As illustrated in Fig. 4B, the formed NGQDs-apt/UiO-66-NH2 composites showed a decrease in fluorescence intensity as the adsorption time increased, stabilizing around 8 min. Therefore, 8 min was identified as the optimal adsorption time for subsequent detection processes.

Furthermore, we delved into the aptamer binding specificity towards its target OTC, over time-dependent reactions to achieve effective fluorescence recovery. To assess this, we maintained a constant OTC concentration of 10 μM and examined how incubation time influenced the fluorescent recovery within NGQDs-apt/UiO-66-NH2 composites (Fig. 4C). Upon adding OTC, we observed a gradual increase in the aptasensor's fluorescence response over a period of 40 min, followed by stabilization. Consequently, 40 min was selected as the optimal incubation duration for specific aptamer-target interaction. We further evaluated the impact of incubation temperature on fluorescent recovery within NGQDs-apt/UiO-66-NH2 composites when exposed to OTC across four distinct temperatures: 4, 25, 37, and 55 °C (Fig. 4D). The results indicated that an incubation temperature of 37 °C yielded the maximum fluorescence recovery, making it the ideal temperature for detecting OTC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the fluorescence aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal conditions, fluorescence responses at 435 nm wavelength were employed to test the performance of the proposed aptasensor for detecting OTC. As shown as Fig. 5A, with the increasing of the OTC concentrations, the fluorescence intensity at the emission wavelength of 435 nm continuously increased. Fig. 5B showed that there was a good linear correlation between OTC concentration and the fluorescence intensity at the emission wavelength of 435 nm. When the OTC concentration reached 200 μM, the fluorescence intensity stabilized and displayed minimal variation thereafter. Inset of Fig. 5B revealed the calibration curve of the of the aptasensor for OTC detection. This relationship could be described by the regression equation I = 322.9logc OTC + 1182.1, in the range of 1 nM–200 μM with the correlation coefficient R 2 of 0.998. Based on a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3, the limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 0.25 nM. To assess the performance of the developed aptasensor in comparison to other reported aptasensors, we have summarized the comparison results which shown as Table 1 . It was noted that the developed aptasensor provided a wider linear range and a lower LOD than most previously reported aptasensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, repeatability, stability, and practicability of the aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is a pivotal parameter in evaluating the effectiveness of the aptasensor. Maintaining the OTC concentration at 1 μM, the developed aptasensor was used to test the fluorescence responses of other interfering antibiotics such as TET, doxycycline (DOX), lomefloxacin (LOM), and ofloxacin (OFLX) at the concentration of 100 μM. As illustrated in Fig. 5C, significant fluorescence response was only observed when OTC was present, while other interfering antibiotics exhibited minimal responses. The composition of the matrix in cereals, food, or feed products is complex. To further evaluate the selectivity of the developed aptasensor, we added several signature substances such as vitamin C (VC), vitamin B1 (VB1), and vitamin B6 (VB6) and typical metal ions such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, and Fe3+ as interfering substances. As can be seen form Fig. S4, the developed aptasensor exhibited significantly higher fluorescence intensity when incubated with 10 μM OTC compared to samples containing only interfering substances. This observation indicated the developed aptasensor had good selectivity. We examined the correlation between estimated and adding concentrations of OTC detected to validate the accuracy of the developed aptasensor. As depicted in Fig. 5D, the slope was 0.95 which approached to 1.0, suggesting that the developed aptasensor possessed good accuracy. The repeatability of the developed aptasensor was measured at the OTC concentration of 1 μM (Fig. 5E) for through six repeated was 0.70 %, indicating this aptasensor possessed excellent repeatability. The aptasensor's long-term stability was evaluated by assessing the fluorescence response every two days when stored at 4 °C and bound with 10 μM OTC. Fig. 5F indicated that the developed aptasensor still possessed 91.8 % of the primal intensdity after 14 days, manifesting a good long-term stability held by the developed aptasensor.

To further appraise the practical application of the developed aptasensor, it was adopted for using the standard addition method to detect the OTC in chicken. The sample was prepared according to the previously reported procedure (Supporting Information). Three different concentrations of OTC (5, 8, and 10 μM) were added to the pretreatment solution for the chicken sample. The results presented in Table S3 indicated that the recovery rates ranged from 99.0 % to 108.4 %, with the relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 2.09 %. These findings suggested that the developed aptasensor had potential practicality in complex matrices.

We have developed a fluorescence aptasensor for the detection of OTC, employing NGQDs and UiO-66-NH2 as the energy donor and acceptor, respectively. The intrinsic amino groups of UiO-66-NH2 facilitate the adsorption of NGQDs-labeled aptamers through hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking interactions, thereby promoting fluorescence quenching. Specifically, the specificity of the aptamer for OTC induces detachment of the NGQDs-aptamer complex from UiO-66-NH2, effectively separating the fluorescent donor from its acceptor and resulting in a recovery of NGQDs fluorescence. This methodology successfully enables sensitive detection of OTC. Our study demonstrates that the fluorescence intensity of NGQDs exhibits a robust linear correlation with OTC concentration over a range spanning 0.001–200 μM, with a detection limit established at 0.25 nM (S/N = 3). This sensor system provides an extensive linear range, low detection limit, high sensitivity, and excellent selectivity. Notably, it has been successfully applied to detect OTC in real chicken samples.
